text: Welcome to another podcast from School of Surgery. Today we're going to talk about anti-reflux surgery. I'm Keaton Jones, Academic Clinical Fellow in General Surgery at the Oxford Deanery. Today's learning outcomes include knowledge of the relevant surgical anatomy, key operative steps of the most frequently performed of the anti-reflux operations, the Nissen three hundred and sixty degree fundoplication, knowledge of alternative anti-reflux laparoscopic procedures, and recollection of the important complications of anti-reflux surgery.

The laparoscopic view shows the initial anatomy with the liver, stomach, greater omentum, and lesser omentum visible. The liver is retracted to reveal the spleen, anterior vagus, right and left crura of the diaphragm, greater curve, fundus, angle of His, lesser curve, and pylorus, which are key anatomical structures for the fundoplication procedure.

The patient is positioned appropriately for the fundoplication procedure, and laparoscopic ports are inserted. The liver is retracted to expose the surgical field, followed by the initial peritoneal dissection. The short gastric vessels are divided, and dissection around the esophagus and crura is performed to create a posterior window. Suturing is then used for crural repair, culminating in the formation of a 360-degree wrap around the esophagus.

The patient is positioned in an armchair-like position on a specialized operating table, with knees split and flexed, hips flexed to approximately ten degrees, and the table tilted head-up. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, facing directly towards them for port insertion.

The laparoscopic setup for a Nissen fundoplication involves the placement of a minimum of five ports. A ten millimeter camera port is positioned a hand breadth below the xiphoid process, slightly left of the midline. A ten millimeter Babcock retractor port is also used. The abdomen is insufflated using a Veress needle at Palmer's point, followed by the insertion of a five millimeter port at the same site. An epigastric port is utilized for the liver retractor, and two additional five millimeter ports are placed for the surgeon's instruments.

The surgeon inserts a 5mm epigastric port, which is then removed to allow the placement of a Nathanson liver retractor. The retractor, resembling a shepherd's hook, is inserted hook side first into the abdomen. It applies smooth and equal pressure under the left hemiliver, retracting it superiorly to expose the lesser omentum, stomach, and greater omentum. The retractor is then secured to a table fixation device, providing a clear view of the operative area.

The surgeon performs peritoneal dissection through the lesser omentum, using a laparoscopic Babcock retractor to hold the fundus of the stomach to the patient's left. The lesser omentum is lifted, revealing a bloodless window that allows dissection towards the crura of the diaphragm. The peritoneum overlying the anterior border of the esophagus is divided, exposing the right crus of the diaphragm. Care is taken to avoid damaging the anterior division of the vagus nerve. The fundus of the stomach is mobilized by dividing the greater omentum from the greater curve, including the division of the short gastric vessels. The assistant retracts the greater omentum to the left while the surgeon retracts the stomach to the right, using diathermy to carefully divide the short gastric vessels to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection between the left cruce of the diaphragm and the anterior aspect of the oesophagus, developing a plane for the posterior window. Tonsil swabs are used as dissection tools to aid in this process. The window opens up behind the oesophagus, allowing the fundus of the stomach to be pulled behind it for the Nissen 360-degree wrap. Final strands of peritoneum overlying the oesophagus are divided to ensure mobility for the wrap.

The surgeon uses a tonsil swab as a blunt dissector to develop the plane around the oesophagus, both posteriorly and anteriorly, while carefully avoiding the nearby heart and lungs to prevent entering the pleural cavity.

The surgeon carefully develops the posterior window, taking care to avoid damaging the posterior division of the vagus nerve, which is visible and pointed out by the surgeon. A Jake's catheter is passed through the posterior window and secured anteriorly with a Babcock retractor, allowing the assistant to manipulate the top of the stomach and oesophagus for better access to the posterior window for further dissection.

The surgeon performs the crural repair by suturing the left and right crura together to reduce the hiatus and prevent a hiatus hernia. The assistant retracts the oesophagus laterally to provide a clear view of the crura. The needle is passed through the left and right crura in one pass, and the knot is tied according to the surgeon's preference. The repair is adjusted to avoid being too tight, preventing dysphagia, with the aid of a bougie passed down the oesophagus under direct vision.

The surgeon completes the crural repair using three sutures, with the final suture being tied at the top of the visual field. The next stage involves creating a 360-degree wrap. The surgeon identifies the fundus of the stomach, ensuring its mobility, and uses a Johan instrument to pass through the posterior window behind the esophagus. The fundus is grasped and pulled through the window, performing the shoeshine maneuver to ensure adequate mobility. The fundus is then positioned behind the esophagus, and the first suture is placed to secure the fundus over the esophagus, with the assistant maintaining tension using a Jake's catheter.

The surgeon expertly completes the fundoplication by applying the third and final suture, securing the 360-degree wrap of the stomach fundus around the oesophagus. The wrap is anchored anteriorly with three interrupted sutures, forming a complete encirclement of the oesophagus.

As mentioned at the outset of this screencast, in the learning objectives, I will briefly mention the alternative procedures which are sometimes performed much less frequently than the NISN, as it has the most robust body of evidence for its long-term efficacy. However, the alternatives, one is a Dawes wrap, which is anterior, this is where the fundus is brought anterior over the esophagus, therefore you do not need to make the posterior window and then sutured onto the stomach or esophagus anteriorly. The toupee is the same principle as a NISN-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M For various reasons, the Nissen has been adopted as the most common approach and is certainly the first line when combining the operation with a hiatus hernia repair.

In terms of complications, I will mention the specific intraoperative complications related to the Nissen fundoplication. As you can see through the operative videos, we're operating very close to the spleen, especially when dividing the short gastrics, and therefore bleeding from the spleen or these vessels is a potential complication. Also visceral perforation, which includes the stomach and the oesophagus. And then finally, when performing the hiatal dissection, there is a possibility of entering the pleural cavity, resulting in pneumothoraces. In terms of post-operative complications, one of the most frequently reported is gas bloat. This is where patients are unable to pass gas by the oral route and therefore they feel an element of discomfort, like their stomach has become bloated. Equally, depending on the technique used, if the wrap is too tight or the accrual repair is too tight, patients can suffer with dysphagia. Again, in relation to the gas bloat, as gas is not travelling upwards, it travels downwards, and so some patients complain of troublesome flatulence. And finally, probably the most common over the long term, certainly after ten years, a significant proportion of patients, and when I say significant, maybe up towards ten, twenty percent, will report recurrent symptoms.

And this is most frequently identified by asking them whether they're back on their anti-acid medications, such as a H-II antagonist or a proton pump inhibitor. So going back to our learning outcomes, hopefully by the end of this screencast, you will now have knowledge of the relevant surgical anatomy. You'll be aware of the key operative steps involved in a Nissen, three hundred and sixty degree fundoplication. Briefly, an overview of the alternative laparoscopic antireflux procedures and recall the important complications of antireflux surgery.

So thank you very much for joining us for this new podcast, Anti-Reflux Surgery. Remember you can follow us on Facebook by searching School of Surgery, on iTunes or via schoolofsurgery.podomatic.com. I'd like to thank Mr. Sean Appleton who performed the surgery for this podcast and he was assisted by both myself and surgical care practitioner Glynis Howard. Thank you very much.
